content: When you call `cargo build` on a project with a `build.rs`, the compiler doesn't just compile your Rust code; it executes that script before running `rustc`, allowing you to inspect system headers, run external commands, or parse configuration files. Inside this hook, bindgen is often used to generate safe Rust bindings from C headers by parsing them into `proc-macro` attributes attached to your Rust types. The core mechanism relies on a two-pass approach where bindgen reads the raw file content into memory, tokenizes it using its internal parser which handles multi-line declarations and comments, then recursively builds an Abstract Syntax Tree that maps every macro, function pointer, and struct definition. You might wonder why we bother with this generation step when we could just write the bindings manually; the answer is that maintaining large C APIs in Rust becomes a nightmare of drift as the upstream changes, while bindgen generates code from source, ensuring your FFI layer stays synchronized without manual edits. When you invoke `cargo build`, the build script runs before compilation, so any errors in parsing or configuration will halt the process and return a non-zero exit status that stops `rustc` entirely. Bindgen’s output is structured around a strict type mapping system where it converts C types like `int32_t` to Rust primitives such as `i32`, but you must understand that this mapping isn't automatic for every case; it relies on heuristics and rules defined in its configuration files or command-line flags. For instance, if your header uses a custom typedef for an integer width, bindgen might generate a distinct `type` alias in Rust unless you explicitly override the default behavior via arguments like `--allow-list` to permit specific types into the final crate output. The real power emerges when you combine this with Rust’s safety guarantees; by default, bindgen generates code that implements traits like `Copy`, `Deref`, or custom `From` conversions to ensure zero-cost abstractions over C pointers and structs. You can block unwanted declarations entirely using a blacklist mechanism where names starting with certain prefixes are ignored, or conversely use an allowlist to restrict the generated bindings to only those types you care about, preventing accidental exposure of internal implementation details through the FFI boundary. Even when generating safe wrappers, bindgen creates code that respects Rust’s ownership model by wrapping C pointers in smart pointers like `Box` or `Rc`, ensuring they don't escape into the FFI layer improperly unless explicitly exposed via traits like `Send` and `Sync`. If you need to handle complex nested structures in your header files, you can customize how bindgen maps them using its configuration options which define the behavior for specific patterns like enum variants or function pointer signatures. The final result is a generated `lib.rs` file that you drop into your project directory; when `cargo build` runs again, Rust compiles this file alongside your application code, linking against the C libraries defined in the headers you parsed earlier. This entire pipeline—from header parsing to AST construction to type mapping—is what makes `build.rs` with bindgen a robust solution for maintaining large-scale FFI interfaces without manual maintenance overhead. If your upstream C API changes frequently, simply rerun `cargo build`, and the next iteration of `bindgen` will regenerate the bindings based on the new headers, automatically updating types like `Option<T>` or `Result<A,B>` to match the C definitions while keeping your Rust code stable.
